seoTitle: How to Attach Documents in Microsoft Loop (2026)
metaDescription: Upload or link Word, Excel, PDF, and other files inside Microsoft Loop pages using inline smart cards or the workspace sidebar uploader.
intro: Reference material scattered across disconnected folders makes it hard for your team to find what they need. This guide covers two ways to attach documents in Microsoft Loop — pasting a cloud file link to create an interactive preview card, and uploading a file directly through the workspace sidebar.

steps.heading: Copy a shared file link
steps.body: Open your cloud storage app, locate your file, and copy its shared link so you can paste it into Loop.

steps.heading: Paste it as an inline smart card
steps.body: Place your cursor on the canvas, paste the document link, and let Loop auto-convert it into a live preview card.

steps.heading: Or upload directly from the sidebar
steps.body: Move to the left side panel, click Create New, and choose the Upload a File utility to bring a document in from your computer.

steps.heading: Select your file
steps.body: Choose your PDF or Office document from the system file picker and click Open to drop it into your navigation tree.

steps.heading: Manage permissions
steps.body: Click the attached file's sharing gear to make sure your team has the right read or edit access.

faq.question: Where are uploaded files actually stored?
faq.answer: Files uploaded through the sidebar are stored inside personal workspace directories connected to OneDrive for Business.

faq.question: Is there a file size limit for attachments?
faq.answer: Loop is designed to store external documents at sizes well beyond typical office files, but very large files should still be checked against your organization's storage limits.

faq.question: Can I rename an attached file inside Loop?
faq.answer: Yes, you can use the sidebar option menu on an attached file to rename its display label without renaming the source file itself.

faq.question: Why can't my teammate open a file I attached?
faq.answer: This is usually a permissions issue — check the file's sharing settings to make sure your team has read or edit access.
